Q1: I organized my study routine around listening to lectures and audio materials, since that's how I learn best. I'd usually set aside blocks of time during the week, maybe around 11 hours total, where I'd listen to recorded lectures while doing other things or sitting somewhere quiet. I kept a loose schedule but tried to be consistent because with my anxiety levels, having some structure helps me feel less overwhelmed. I'd often record myself summarizing concepts and play those back later.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to keep regular times, but honestly when deadlines approached, everything shifted into crisis mode. The stress would spike and I'd have to adjust my whole week around getting assignments done. I think I managed to complete almost everything on time, but the routine was more of a guideline than a strict schedule.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something due soon or when I needed to check announcements. Sometimes I'd go in just to download materials so I could study them offline later. With my anxiety, I'd sometimes log in just to reassure myself I hadn't missed anything important. It wasn't always about actively studying—sometimes it was just checking that everything was okay.
Q4: Honestly, logging into the LMS never really became automatic for me. I'd remember to check it a few times a week, but it wasn't like brushing my teeth or anything. What became routine was downloading audio files and lecture recordings early in the week so I could listen to them on my own time. The LMS was more of a starting point than where I actually spent my study time.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could listen to them multiple times. I'd watch them once for the visual content, then convert key parts to audio or just replay them while doing other things. The assignment submission portal was also essential—knowing exactly where to submit and seeing confirmation helped reduce my anxiety about whether things went through properly. Having everything organized in one place was helpful even if I didn't use it all online.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums. I know I had zero posts, and that's because participating in written discussions online makes my anxiety worse. I'd rather talk to peers in person during my extracurricular activities. Some of the supplementary readings I also skipped if they weren't directly tied to assignments because I had to manage my energy and focus on what counted.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. I also valued anything I could engage with through listening rather than just reading. If something felt like busywork or wouldn't impact my grade, I'd deprioritize it because I was already stretched thin managing my stress levels.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required work had clear stakes—if I didn't do it, my grade suffered. Optional stuff, even if interesting, got pushed aside because I was already anxious about completing the mandatory work. There's only so much mental energy I have, and the required tasks consumed most of it.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or exercising. I also used textbooks and external resources like YouTube videos that explained concepts in audio-friendly ways. My notes were handwritten and I'd sometimes record myself reading them aloud to review later. The LMS was just the gateway to materials I'd use elsewhere.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd spend hours listening to downloaded lectures on repeat, working through problems in my notebook, or discussing concepts with peers from my extracurricular group. None of that showed up as login time or clicks. My actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then study it offline for days. I might watch a lecture video once online but listen to the audio version five more times on my phone. The LMS would only show that single access, not the hours I spent with that material afterward.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when multiple deadlines converged or when my anxiety spiked. Sometimes I'd feel so stressed that I couldn't focus even when I sat down to study. The CBT sessions I had helped me work through some of that, teaching me to recognize when I was catastrophizing. But those rough patches meant I'd disengage from the LMS temporarily even while still trying to keep up with coursework.
Q13: Workload and stress hugely affected my LMS usage. When I was overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in because seeing all the tasks made my anxiety worse. I'd work from downloaded materials instead so I didn't have to face the platform. Fatigue was also real—after a long day, the last thing I wanted was more screen time, so I'd switch to audio-based studying instead.
Q14: Sometimes I'd log in and just scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My mind would be racing with anxious thoughts, and even though my eyes were on the screen, nothing was sticking. Those sessions felt wasted, but I was going through the motions hoping something would click. It's frustrating when your brain won't cooperate even when you're trying.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts because the thought of writing something public made me anxious. I'd also delay starting big assignments because the fear of not doing well enough was paralyzing. I knew they were important, but my anxiety would convince me I wasn't ready, and I'd end up cramming closer to the deadline. The CBT helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was still hard.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I'd probably drift. As due dates approached, my LMS activity would spike because I'd be checking requirements, submitting work, and making sure I hadn't missed anything. The structure deadlines provided was actually helpful for someone like me—it forced action even when anxiety was telling me to freeze.
Q17: Feedback motivated me when it was constructive and specific. Good feedback made me want to engage more with the next assignment. But waiting for feedback was stressful, and sometimes the delay made me anxious about whether I was on the right track. When I got positive responses, it boosted my confidence and made me more willing to tackle the next challenge.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my week. I appreciated knowing what was expected and when. But the structure didn't account for different learning styles—there wasn't much audio content provided, so I had to create my own. The attendance tracking was also stressful since my percentage was low, even though I was keeping up with the material in my own way.
Q19: Having high-quality video lectures that I could download made engagement easier because I could adapt them to my auditory learning style. What made it harder was the expectation of discussion forum participation—that format just doesn't work for me. Also, not having reliable internet access meant I couldn't always engage with live or streaming content, so downloadable resources were essential.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding without finding it. They're clicking around, rewatching videos, looking at different resources—all trying to grasp something that isn't clicking. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Or maybe they're anxious like me and keep checking things without actually being able to focus and complete work.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was moderate at best, but I performed reasonably well because my real learning happened offline. I'd download materials, study them extensively away from the platform, and then just log in to submit. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the course content.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when you submitted work and maybe patterns around deadlines. But they miss everything that happens offline—the listening, the note-taking, the discussions with peers, the mental processing. They also can't capture quality of engagement. Someone might be logged in for hours but zoned out, while another person spends twenty focused minutes and learns more.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material until it makes sense to me. It's not about time spent on a platform—it's about whether I'm thinking critically, making connections, and retaining information. For me, engagement often looks like listening to a lecture while walking, then talking through concepts with friends. It's active mental involvement, not just passive presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety and how much energy I spent managing it while trying to learn. It wouldn't capture the hours of audio-based studying, the peer discussions during extracurricular activities, or the CBT sessions that helped me stay functional. It also wouldn't show my resilience—how I kept pushing through despite high stress and low attendance, still completing nearly all my assignments and earning a Merit grade.
